item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of the best math scips since SCP-033. Fantastic use of esoteric mathematical theory.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love it! it's the Banach Tarski theorem but applied to cutting salmon! reminds me of that famous chocolate bar trick that appears to make chocolate out of nothing but cutting the bar in a certain way, which I'm assuming was the main inspiration. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know what's happening and my brain hurts. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll be honest. Not what I was expecting. Not unhappy though. I was pleasantly surprised to see a math concept thatI'm pretty familiar with used as the basis for an anomaly, and the read kept me interested. I will say the test logs did get a little repetitive, and caused to skim a bit on first read, but overall it's a unique concept executed well, IMO.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There! I have fixed it to become much clearer. Plus, there are the points that they found out that they can use the results to enhance containment chambers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm generally pretty down for what's going on in this article, but it's not really stated why this research would be beneficial to containment at large. I'm guessing it has to do with "reversing conceptual damage", but I'd prefer the reasoning behind the Foundation repeatedly "poking the bear" be more explicit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm, that's a good point, since it did look jammed a bit.

As for the test logs, they were supposed to be logically connected to each other, while the ending is there so that people wouldn't think that the SCP would just become weaponized by the Foundation. I will still work on improving some of them, though:)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn’t even hate the pun, it could probably work in different context. But it’s out of place just being tossed in the middle of the article, y’know?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fair points, especially on the frying pun. I'm going to try to improve some of the test logs here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really wanted to like this the description is fabulous in how it distilled a very complicated concept to comprehensible terms. But I feel like the test logs squander that potential, they’re all okay enough but don’t play with the ideas presented earlier and the fish frying pun was groan-worthy. It also felt weird that the article kept going after that note which felt like a somewhat appropriate ending. If the testing was made a little more robust this could be great. As it is I found it to be a letdown.

item.title: salmon is yummy with math.
item.content:encoded:
Heya, Eze here with a very experimental style of article, about math, salmon, and vanity.

Special thanks to ManyMeats, caspian2, cybersqyd, RockTeethMothEyes, and DrUnoriginal for critting and helping make this work.

Image Credits: Australian Salmon. Tasmanian Archives and Heritage Office -> Public Domain.
https://www.flickr.com/photos/107895189@N03/15211549890/in/photolist-pbcaPy-29eeGFn-p9irSG
